coredata.dc:description: Due to their unique properties, carbon dots have attracted significant interest across diverse fields. However, obstacles such as uneven size distribution in the course of synthesis and excessive aggregation lead to fluorescence quenching during solidification process. Moreover, conventional doping procedures frequently yield carbon dot films with constrained functionality, inferior durability, and harsh environments, thereby restricting their practical applications. To address these issues, this study fabricates N-doped carbon dots via a straightforward hydrothermal approach and incorporates them into CMC films by metathesis with CeCl3·7H2O, resulting in a unique carbon dot-encapsulated film. This film overcomes the problem of fluorescence quenching caused by excessive aggregation of carbon dots and allows for size control by adjusting the concentration of Ce(III). The resulting film achieves extreme transparency and superior performance compared to existing UV-blocking films. In addition, the film exhibits exceptional resistance to acids and bases, bleaching, excellent mechanical properties, and good thermal and water stability. Based on these properties, the film achieves size-controlled in-situ photoluminescence, full UV shielding, Cu2+ ion detection, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption. The water-processable and water-adhesive characteristics of CMC enable its use in real-life situations, opening the door to wider use of solid-state carbon dots for multifunctional and intelligent applications.

head.ce:title: Carboxymethylcellulose and rare earth metal cation self-assembly: Tunable encapsulation of carbon dots in a multifunctional transparent film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jinan University, Panyu Campus, Guangzhou 511443, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jinan University, Panyu Campus, Guangzhou 511443, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their unique properties, carbon dots have attracted significant interest across diverse fields. However, obstacles such as uneven size distribution in the course of synthesis and excessive aggregation lead to fluorescence quenching during solidification process. Moreover, conventional doping procedures frequently yield carbon dot films with constrained functionality, inferior durability, and harsh environments, thereby restricting their practical applications. To address these issues, this study fabricates N-doped carbon dots via a straightforward hydrothermal approach and incorporates them into CMC films by metathesis with CeCl3·7H2O, resulting in a unique carbon dot-encapsulated film. This film overcomes the problem of fluorescence quenching caused by excessive aggregation of carbon dots and allows for size control by adjusting the concentration of Ce(III). The resulting film achieves extreme transparency and superior performance compared to existing UV-blocking films. In addition, the film exhibits exceptional resistance to acids and bases, bleaching, excellent mechanical properties, and good thermal and water stability. Based on these properties, the film achieves size-controlled in-situ photoluminescence, full UV shielding, Cu2+ ion detection, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption. The water-processable and water-adhesive characteristics of CMC enable its use in real-life situations, opening the door to wider use of solid-state carbon dots for multifunctional and intelligent applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available in the Supplementary Material of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), zero-dimensional particles below 10 nm, exhibit remarkable chemical stability, tunable luminescence, eco-friendliness, and non-toxicity, attracting wide interest for diverse applications such as luminescence, sensing, imaging, catalysis, and anti-counterfeiting [1]. CQDs synthesis can be categorized into “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches, including discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, hydrothermal synthesis, ultrasonic/microwave synthesis, and others. Among them, bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis has advantages such as low cost, ease of preparation, environmental friendliness, and simplicity, making it a potential candidate for industrialization of CQDs [2]. However, this hydrothermal method is highly susceptible to reaction conditions such as time, temperature, etc., resulting in uneven CQD sizes. Similar problems are also observed in other synthetic methods under uncontrolled conditions. Because most CQDs exhibit quantum size effects, this size heterogeneity can affect their performance [3]. In addition, the solidification of CQDs presents challenges. Encapsulating CQDs within a solid matrix can help them accumulate and further restrict their movement. However, excessive aggregation of CQDs can lead to a decrease in fluorescence intensity, a phenomenon known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [4]. To address this issue, Tang et al. designed solid-state CQDs with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effects, paving a new path for solid-state CQDs [5]. In addition, Shen et al. embedded CQDs in situ into a trisodium citrate crystal matrix, using the spatial confinement of the matrix to suppress the ACQ phenomenon [6]. Tian et al. used an aqueous solution of sodium silicate as a matrix, which underwent a dehydration reaction with surface functional groups, fixing the CQDs through crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) and restricting their molecular motion [7]. Most solid-state CQDs obtained through these methods are in powder form and often require further processing, such as forming films. However, most reported CQD films are obtained by doping, such as mixing CQDs with PVA or coating CQD solutions directly onto polymer substrates [8–10]. These methodologies produce films that exhibit significant concentration dependence, featuring a sharp decrease in transparency at elevated concentrations, and afford limited solidification effects for CQDs. Moreover, poor mechanical properties and water solubility restrict their broader application. Indeed, most CQD studies concentrate on synthesis and preparation rather than application, particularly in solid-state CQDs functionality. Therefore, the potential for versatile applications of CQDs/solid-state CQDs merits further investigation to enhance their value [11–14].

For ultraviolet (UV) absorbing films, shielding performance and transparency are two of the most important parameters. Moreover, high transparency is essential for visual applications such as imaging, masks, windows, and food packaging, and has become a vital trend in film design. Although many reports have documented a plethora of organic and inorganic nanomaterials for UV shielding, they either suffer from poor transparency and significant color differences or incomplete UV band protection. Thus, achieving high UV shielding performance alongside high transparency remains a significant challenge [15]. Moreover, high transparency is essential for visual applications such as imaging, masks, windows, and food packaging, and has become a vital trend in film design [15–17].

In this work, rare earth metal cerium (III) ions and carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) were used for the first time for self-assembly to encapsulate carbon dots, which were obtained from citric acid as the precursor, doped with ethylenediamine, through a simple and inexpensive hydrothermal method. The “bottom-up” hydrothermal method yields carbon dots between 2 and 5 nm with a narrow size distribution. The modified carbon dot shell carries amine functional group chains for coordination with Ce(III) ions. Notably, Ce(III) ions can bond with carboxyl groups in CMC, anchoring the carbon dots within the CMC matrix via ionic bonds. This ionic bond is much stronger than those of intermolecular forces and hydrogen bonds, suppressing the rotation and vibration of carbon dots efficiently. Simultaneously, the film maintains excellent dispersibility and high intensity in situ fluorescence emission at high concentrations. Furthermore, good hydrophilic CMC metathesis with Ce(III) ions forms a water-insoluble CMC−Ce(III) complex, effectively encapsulating the carbon dots. Further increasing the concentration of Ce(III) ions enables the regulation and confinement of carbon dots via coordination with CMC, addressing the disadvantages of creating uneven sizes in synthesis of carbon dot. With these features, we have developed a high-transparency, water-plasticizable film that integrates photoluminescence imaging, complete UV shielding, copper ion detection, three-dimensional anti-counterfeiting, and dual information encryption. Moreover, the functionalized carbon dot film exhibits extreme durability, excellent stability against acids and bases, high temperatures, and high humidity, expanding the scope of application. Simultaneously, the application of its outstanding mechanical properties and water processability has brought it into our daily life, revealing huge potential value in the application of carbon dots.

Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O, ≥99.5 %) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China); Carboxymethylcellulose (600–3000 MPa·s, USP: 300–800), Cerium (III) chloride heptahydrate (CeCl3·7H2O), CuSO4·5H2O, FeCl3, CrCl3, CoCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, CaCl3, ZnSO4 were all purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Ethylenediamine (C2N2H8) and glycerol (C3H8O3) were both purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China).

In this work, three types of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized for comparison. 0.3 g of citric acid (CA) was added to a 15 mL reaction tube, followed by 4 mL of deionized water, denoted as CQD. In a separate reaction, 0.3 g of CA and 0.15 mL of glycerol were added to a 15 mL reaction tube, followed by 4 mL of deionized water, denoted as HO−CQD. Similarly, 0.3 g of CA and 0.15 mL of ethylenediamine were added to a 15 mL reaction tube, followed by 4 mL of deionized water, denoted as N–CQD. Each of the aforementioned solutions was stirred until dissolved homogeneously and then subjected to hydrothermal reaction in an oil bath at a temperature of 156 °C. The reaction was terminated when the solution turned dark brown, yielding the corresponding products. The solutions were then purified and sealed for storage, ready for subsequent use.

2 g of carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) was added to 100 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. The different CQD products obtained from the previous step were each dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and gradually added to the CMC solution (4 wt%). After uniform distribution, 30 mL of the resulting solution was poured into a glass dish with a diameter of 90 mm and left to dry at low temperature until film formation

2 g CMC was added to 100 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. The CMC solution was then poured into a glass dish with a diameter of 90 mm and dried at low temperature until film formation. The CQDs obtained previously were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and mixed with different concentrations of CeCl3·7H2O under constant stirring. The resulting mixture was then poured into the glass dish containing the dried CMC film. After 1.5 h, the solution was poured out, and the film was washed with deionized water and dried to obtain the Ce@CQD/CMC film

The morphological structure of CQDs in the film samples was observed using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL/JEM-1400Flash, Japan). The film samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (miniflex600, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (PerkinElmer spectrum two, USA) was used to characterize the film samples, with a range of 4000–400 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution) was used to characterize the films, with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm and a range of 500–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, UK) was used to analyze the elemental distribution on the surface of the film samples. The structure of the films was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku miniflex600, Japan). The particle size of CQDs was evaluated using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (NanoZS Malvern, UK). The PL intensity and fluorescence lifetime of CQDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FS5, UK), and the absolute PLQY of solid films was calculated using an integrated sphere. 10 mg of the film sample together with aqua regia dissolved in deionized water and the volume of the solution was fixed to 100 mL. The content of Ce (III) ion was measured using an inductively coupled plasma spectrometer (ICAP 7000 SERIES, Thermo). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs in solution was calculated using quinine sulfate (QY=54 %) in 0.2 M H2SO4 as a reference. The equation is as follows [18]:

The films were cut into 70 × 20 mm, with the thickness of CMC films being 0.071 ± 5 mm, and the thickness of other films being 0.062 ± 7 mm. The mechanical strength of the film samples was tested using an electronic tensile tester (Shimadzu AGS-X, Japan) with a gauge length of 43.7 mm. Each sample was tested at least three times, and the average value was taken. The films were subjected to a load-bearing test using weights (totaling 2 kg). The thermal stability of the film samples were analyzed using a thermogravimetric-differential scanning calorimeter (TGA-DSC) (TG209F3-ASC, Germany) under a nitrogen atmosphere from 30 to 600 °C, with a flow rate of 10 mL/min. The color difference parameters of thin film samples were measured using Spectrocolormeter (CHNSpec DS-200, Hangzhou), where L* represents brightness, a* represents red to green, and b* represents yellow to blue. The absorbance (Abs) and transmittance (T %) of the film samples were tested using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Puxi TU-1810PC/SPC, China) with a wavelength range of 190 to 800 nm. To explore the effect of the concentration of CeCl3·7H2O, films with different concentrations of Ce ions were tested. To investigate the impact of different film thicknesses on UV absorption, film samples with varying thicknesses were prepared. The thickness of the films was measured using a spiral micrometer (DL321025S, China), and the corresponding UV transmittance was recorded. To study the stability of UV absorption of the film samples under different pH conditions, the films were placed in environments with varying pH (1.0–11.0) for a period of time, and their UV transmittance was tested. To explore the thermal stability of the film samples, the films were heated at 120 °C for 2 h before undergoing UV shielding tests. The UV shielding efficiency (E %) was calculated using the following equation [20]:

The contact angle of the film samples was measured using a contact angle goniometer (DSA-100, Germany). The contact phase was H2O, and the surrounding phase was air. The sessile drop method was used, and the fitting model was an ellipse. Each sample was measured five times, and the average value was taken. The film samples were dried at 30 °C for 24 h, and the initial mass was recorded. The film samples were then placed in a glass dish and immersed in 50 mL deionized water. After 1 h, the film samples were removed from the solution, and excess water on the surface was wiped off with filter paper. The mass was then measured and recorded. Finally, the film samples were dried until the weight was stable, and the final mass was recorded. The test was repeated three times, and the average value was calculated. the swelling degree (SD %) of the film was calculated using the following equation [21]:

Where

is the mass obtained by weighing.

The water vapor permeability (WVP) of the film samples was assessed by weight loss analysis (wet cup method) according to ASTM E96-95 [22]. 20 mL deionized water was added to the test cup, and the film sample was placed on top of the test cup and tightly sealed. The setup was then placed in a humidity chamber (21 °C, 70 % RH). The cup was weighed every hour for 8 h until a steady state was reached. The slope of the weight loss curve, WVTR (g/m2·s), was calculated, and the WVP (g·m/m2·Pa·s) of the film sample was then calculated using the following formula:
(7)
WVP =
Δ w ∙ L
Δ t ∙ A ∙ Δ p
where w is the weight of the test cup, t is time, A is the area of water vapor transmission, L is the film thickness, and w is the water vapor pressure difference.

Different metal ions (K+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+) at a concentration of 10 μM were added dropwise to the film samples. After waiting for 30 s, the excess liquid on the surface was removed, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer. Sensitivity tests were conducted on the films using different concentrations (0–10 μM) of Cu2+ solution. The excitation wavelength was 352 nm, and the emission wavelength ranged from 400 to 500 nm. The emission intensity at 452 nm was recorded as the response point. At least four parallel tests were repeated, and the average value was taken. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the following formula:
(8)
LOD =
3 δ
K
Where δ Is the standard deviation of the sample, and K is the slope of the fluorescence intensity concentration curve.

In this study, N-CQD represents nitrogen-doped carbon dots, CMC stands for pure carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), and CQD/CMC refers to the films made by doping CMC with a 4 wt% N-CQD aqueous solution. The 0.4Ce@CQD/CMC films were prepared by adding 0.42 g/20 mL of CeCl3·7H2O to the N–CQD aqueous solution (The content of Ce(III) in the film is about 0.187 mmol/g), while Ce@CQD/CMC and 0.8Ce@CQD/CMC films were obtained by adding 0.84 g/20 mL of CeCl3·7H2O (The content of Ce(III) in the film is about 0.225 mmol/g). The 1.2Ce@CQD/CMC films were synthesized by adding 1.22 g/20 mL CeCl3·7H2O to the N–CQD solution (The content of Ce(III) in the film is about 0.243 mmol/g).

The internal structure and composition were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the TEM image of the CQD/CMC film reveals numerous black dots scattered throughout the film, indicating the successful incorporation of CQDs into the CMC film [23]. In the magnified image of CQD/CMC (Fig. 1(b)), CQDs show varying sizes and disordered distribution in the film. The lattice spacing measures 0.33 nm, corresponding to the crystal faces (0 0 2). Generally, the size of carbon quantum dots is less than 10 nm. However, particularly large black dots appear in the image, with particle diameters exceeding 10 nm, suggesting that they are formed by agglomeration of CQDs. However, it is evident that the film produced by this method exhibits poor dispersion. This is attributed to the hydrogen bonding between CQDs and CMC, which hinders the migration of CQDs within the solid matrix. With the increasing presence of CQDs, the available space uneven and decreases, leading to their gradual accumulation and agglomeration. Fig. 1(c) shows the TEM image of the Ce@CQD/CMC film featuring CQDs regularly integrated into gaps, re-organized by the synergistic interactions between Ce(III) ions and CMC. The coordination bonds between Ce(III) and CMC anchored irregularly distributed CQDs within the film, while most locations restrict the free movement of CQDs, which is beneficial to improve the transparency of the film. Fig. 1(d) shows a magnified image of a group of CQDs formed at a low Ce(III) concentration, with an average size of approximately 40 nm, consisting of numerous small CQD particles (ca. 1–2 nm) and large CQD particles (ca. 5 nm). In contrast, Fig. 1(e) shows a magnified image of CQDs generated at a high Ce(III) concentration, with an average size of about 30 nm, predominantly composed of large CQD particles with minute numbers of small CQDs. Both HR-TEM and SEAD highlight a high resolution lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, suggesting that Ce(III) concentration impacts the position of fixed CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1(i), the average particle size of CQDs produced in the low metal ion concentration is around 2–5 nm with a wide size distribution. In contrast, at high metal ion concentrations, the average particle size of CQDs concentrates around 4.5 nm, which may be due to metal ion concentration having an impact on nanoparticle growth. Meanwhile, smaller and regular CQD arrangements can effectively enhance the optical transparency and dispersion of the film [24].

As shown in Fig. 3(f), the FT-IR spectra of the films exhibit a similar pattern. The peaks at 1321 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1 are characteristic of cellulose motif [25]. The peak at 1647 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H bending vibration peak of CQD. The peak at 1587 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of −COO−Mn+(metal ion) in CMC and CQD/CMC films [26]. In the Ce@CQD/CMC films, this peak shifts to 1575 cm−1, indicating the substitution of Na(I) in CMC with Ce(III). The range of 3200 to 3500 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Even with the introduction of CQDs, the patterns remained unchanged, possibly due to the low content of −NH2 groups. It can be observed that the absorption peak of hydroxyl group (−OH) for pure CMC is located at 3342 cm−1, while for CQD/CMC and Ce@CQD/CMC, this peak shifts to 3335 cm−1 and 3331 cm−1, respectively. This redshift indicates the existence of electrostatic interactions and intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the amino (−NH2) groups of the CQDs shell and CMC [27], which is consistent with our hypothesis regarding the cross-linking mechanism of CQD/CMC.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the films are displayed in Fig. 1(g). For the CMC film, only one broad diffraction peak appeared at 2θ ≈ 20.96°, which can be attributed to the crystalline cellulose structure of CMC [28], possessing a lower degree of crystallinity. This characteristic is one reason for the solubility of CMC [29], The XRD pattern of the CQD/CMC film shows no significant difference from that of CMC, with no additional diffraction peaks observed. This may be due to the abundance of amorphous fluorescent small molecules in CQDs, which do not enhance the orderliness of the film [30]. In contrast, Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits a sharp peak at 2θ ≈ 25.03°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) crystal plane [31,32], consistent with the TEM results. Significantly, Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits superior CQD loading than conventional doping methods at the same level of transparency, especially for larger carbon cores, thus enhancing film crystallinity to some extent.

The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of the three films are displayed in Fig. 1(h). As illustrated, the CMC film exhibits peaks corresponding to Na, O, and C elements. In the CQD/CMC film, an additional peak for the N element is observed, originating from the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In the Ce@CQD/CMC film, the peak for Na disappears, replaced by a peak for the Ce element. The C 1s deconvoluted spectrum in Fig. 1(j) reveals three energy peaks at 283.01 eV, 284.72 eV, and 286.33 eV, corresponding to C−H/C−N, C−C, and C−O−C bonds, respectively [33]. The O 1s deconvoluted spectra of both films in Fig. 1(k) exhibit three bond energy peaks. For CQD/CMC, the peaks at 529.79 eV and 531.11 eV correspond to C−O and C=O/O−C−O, respectively. In the case of Ce@CQD/CMC, the peaks are observed at 530.23 eV, and 531.06 eV, with a shift from 534.07 eV to 532.22 eV, attributed to the substitution of −COO−Na+ by (−COO−)3Ce3+ bonds [34]. The N 1s deconvoluted spectrum of CQD/CMC in Fig. 1(l) shows two energy peaks at 396.58 eV and 398.40 eV, corresponding to C−N and N−H bonds, respectively [35]. Additionally, a new peak appears at 401.40 eV in Ce@CQD/CMC, which can be attributed to the complexation between Ce(III) and N. The deconvoluted spectrum in Fig. 1(m) displays five energy peaks for Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 spin–orbit doublets, with fitting peaks at 881.24 eV, 884.94 eV, 899.44 eV, 903.07 eV, and 906.04 eV [36]. Moreover, no peak is observed in the range of 910–920 eV, indicating that the Ce element is in the form of Ce(III) [37], as the reducing ability of CMC prevents the oxidation of Ce(III).

Fig. 2 (a) shows images of the film samples under visible and UV light (365 nm), with CQDs solution in beaker. The transmittance and absorbance of the films in the UV–visible spectrum were measured, as shown in Fig. 2(b and c). In the visible range, light at 550 nm is sensitive to the human eye, and its transmittance (T550) is often used as a clarity indicator for UV shielding efficiency of materials [15]. Due to colorless of Ce(III) and transparent nature, Ce@CQD/CMC film's ΔE reduces with Ce(III) concentration, ultimately attaining near-colorlessness. Fig. 2(e) indicates that all five films exceed 88 % transmittance, with CMC presenting the highest T550 at 91.26 %. Increasing Ce(III) content, the T550 of Ce@CQD/CMC films also increases correspondingly. Further analysis of the film surface using colorimeter reveals that CQD/CMC has the largest total color difference (ΔE) of 27.74, exhibiting a higher red value (a* = 23.09). As seen in Fig. 2(a), CQD/CMC has the darkest appearance despite its high transparency (T550 = 88.11 %). However, the color difference of yellowing can seriously hinder the application [38]. Due to the colorless and transparent nature of Ce(III), the ΔE of Ce@CQD/CMC films decreases with increasing Ce(III) content, making the films near-colorlessness. Subsequently, the UV region was tested and analyzed, as shown in Fig. 2(d) (E represents UV shielding performance, and T represents transmittance). CMC has almost no UV blocking ability in the UV-A and UV-B regions, only with an EUV-C of 39.74 %. CQD/CMC exhibits better performance in the UV-A and UV-C regions (93.14 %, and 94.58 %), while the UV-B region is only 64.46 %. For Ce@CQD/CMC, the best performance is observed at a Ce(III) concentration of 0.8, with UV shielding efficiencies in the UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C regions reaching 99.88 %, 99.12 %, and 99.99 %, respectively. The T400 is only 1.84 %, indicating that the entire UV region achieves a basic full shield of over 99.50 %, demonstrating an exceptional performance. This is due to the fact that at a Ce(III) content of 0.4, the main component of the film is unmodified CQDs; when the content increases to 1.2, an excess of Ce(III) occupies a large number of spatial sites in the film, reducing the amount of CQDs. In addition, 0.8Ce@CQD/CMC possesses an extremely high UV blocking factor (the ratio of transmittance in the visible region to that in the UV region, as shown in Fig. 2(e) [39], which strictly meets the requirement for a high UV blocking rate while further achieving high transparency of the film. In contrast, the doped CQD/CMC, despite having a comparable visible light transmittance (88.11 %, 88.21 %) to Ce@CQD/CMC, performs poorly and exhibits a higher color difference in the appearance evaluation of the film. Further analysis of the films shown in Fig. 2(c) revealed that the absorption at 200–300 nm for both CQD/CMC and Ce@CQD/CMC. This phenomenon is attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2-hybridized carbon core in the CQDs [40]. In addition, Ce@CQD/CMC shows an additional peak in this region due to the absorption of photoelectrons by Ce(III) (as illustrated in Fig. S1(d)) [41]. Consequently, the dual absorption peaks of Ce@CQD/CMC in the 200–300 nm range are broader and higher than the single peak of CQD/CMC, thus enhancing the UV absorption efficiency of the film in the UV-B region. The peak height of 1.2Ce@CQD/CMC falls with elevated Ce(III) content, leading to a reduction in the spatial sites and the content of CQDs. In the UV-A region, Ce@CQD/CMC displays two absorption peaks, one at around 340 nm related to the n-π* transition of the −NH2 groups [42], n-d the other peak near 380 nm arising from the n-π* transition of the sp3-hybridized carbon core [43]. In contrast, CQD/CMC in the UV-A region shows only a single peak, suspected to overlap of absorption for both the −NH2 group and the sp3-hybridized carbon. In short, the incorporation of Ce(III) compensates for the deficiencies in the UV-B performance of CQDs, acting as a dual barrier, thereby achieving complete UV shielding in the film. Moreover, Ce(III) selectively immobilizes CQDs, ensuring their regular distribution, which significantly enhances their dispersion within the film, imparting high transparency to the film.

Moreover, we examined the correlation between film thickness and UV transmittance (Fig. 2(f)). The peak at 320 nm is relatively distinct with thinner films (38 ± 3 μm). However, as the thickness increases, the transmittance gradually decreases, and the change in transmittance saturates when the film thickness reaches 55 ± 6 μm. This is hypothesized to be due to the increase in film thickness, which leads to an increase in the number of −NH2 groups, raising the UV absorption peak and causing a decrease in transmittance and a blue shift of the transmittance peak until largely shielding is achieved [10,44]. Notably, in the range of 38–76 μm, the visible light transmittance of the films does not show a significant decrease with increasing thickness, indicating that the transparency of the films is essentially unaffected.

Photoluminescence studies on the film samples were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The PL curves corresponding to excitation wavelengths (Ex) from 320 to 450 nm were analyzed, with deconvolution applied to their highest peaks. As shown in Fig. 2(j), Gaussian fitting reveals two emission peaks. The earlier peak before 450 nm is due to the emission caused by sp3 hybridization of N-doped fluorescent groups, whereas the later peak after 450 nm is attributed to carbon core-dominated emission, representing a dual emission mechanism [45]. Fig. S4(a) presents the PL spectrum of high-concentration CQD aq., where the emission before Ex = 400 is dominated by N-based fluorescent groups without significant peak shift, while the emission after Ex = 400 is carbon core-dominated with a clear excitation-dependent phenomenon [45]. Fig. 2(h) shows the PL spectrum of the CQD/CMC film, with an optimal excitation wavelength around Ex = 350 for the emission dominated by the sp3 of N-based functional groups, until a redshift of the emission peak occurs at Ex = 390 (from 456 to 468 nm), indicating carbon core-dominated emission. Fig. 2(i) presents the PL spectrum of the Ce@CQD/CMC film, with an optimal excitation wavelength around Ex = 400, and a subsequent redshift of the emission peak (from 460 to 469 nm), also indicating carbon core-dominated emission. However, compared to liquid CQDs, the redshift with increasing excitation wavelength in solid CQDs is significantly reduced, suggesting that solid CQDs have good excitation independence in the carbon core-dominated emission stage, possibly due to solvent relaxation causing a “giant red edge effect” [46].

Furthermore, acid/base resistance plays an important role in the use of films in different environments, and most N-doped CQDs are highly dependent on pH data [47]. Fig. 2(k) illustrates the pH-fluorescence intensity correlation, with pH = 7 setting the standard, and Fig. S3(a, b) displays the PL spectra of the two films with varying pH value. Under strong acidic conditions (pH = 1–2), the fluorescence intensity of both films decreases significantly, widely considered to be a result of protonation, where H+ surrounds the CQD to compete for electrons, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity [48]. Fig. S3(c) reveals the transmittance curves of Ce@CQD/CMC at varying pH values, with a notable increase in UV-B transmittance at pH = 1, possibly caused by the protonation of some carboxyl groups in CMC by hydrogen ions, leading to the detachment of Ce (III). As the pH increases, CQDs undergo deprotonation with OH− to provide electrons to CQDs, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity. At high pH values, the fluorescence intensity decreases due to CQDs was covered extensively by electrons of by OH− [49]. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity change of the surface-protected Ce@CQD/CMC with pH shift is negligible, especially between pH = 2–12, indicating excellent pH stability.

It is noted that the absorption peak of the sp2 structure of the carbon core in Fig. 2(c) shifts with the varying of concentration of Ce(III), and the emission peak of Ce@CQD/CMC in Fig. 2(j) exhibits a redshift compared to CQD/CMC. The quantum confinement effect (QCE) is one of the most fundamental characteristics of carbon dots, which directly determines photoluminescence emission when the size of CQDs is smaller than their exciton Bohr radius [15]. Based on this, we hypothesize that the self-assembly of Ce(III) with CMC can prevent excessive aggregation of CQDs in solids and achieve control over the size of CQDs.

To this end, the relationship between CQD concentration and PL was investigated. Fig. 3 (a, c-h) shows the optimal excitation and emission curves at different CQD concentrations. The concentrations of CQD/CMC and CQD aq. were the same. Due to the different preparation methods of the two films, we use a transmittance of 550 nm (T550) to represent their concentrations. At low concentration (T550 = 90 %), the absorption peaks of CQD aq. and CQD/CMC falls between 360−370 nm, and the solid CQD has a smaller Stokes shift, as shown in Fig. 4 (b). This is due to encapsulation within the CMC solid matrix, stiffening CQD, and resulting in hydrogen bonding from the −NH2 groups at the CQD shell edge to CMC, reducing nonradiative transitions of CQD [4]. With the further increase in CQD (Ce@CQD) concentration (T550 = 83 %), the absorption peaks of CQD aq. and CQD/CMC undergo further redshift. The emission peak of CQD aq. does not further redshift, and the full width at half maxima (FWHM) remains unchanged, as shown in Fig. 4(a), suggesting stable dispersion of CQD in the aqueous environment. As the concentration of CQD(Ce@CQD) increased (T550 = 74 %), the emission peak and FWHM of CQD aq. remained unchanged. At this point, the absorption and emission peaks of CQD/CMC undergo a large range of redshifts, with a broad absorption peak appearing at 320–400 nm, which can be explained by aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [50], where shorter wavelength emission is absorbed by longer wavelength emission, as shown in Fig. 3(i). However, excessive Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) in CQD-doped films is undesirable, often causing a decrease in fluorescence intensity, ACQ, quantum yield, and transparency issues. With the addition of a large amount of CQD (T550 = 65 %), the absorption and emission peaks continue to redshift due to AIE, FWHM increases, the peak shape widens, and the PLQY is only about 1.4 %. In contrast, for Ce@CQD/CMC at this concentration, the absorption peak widens further, and the emission peak blue shifts. This is due to ionic crosslinking between Ce(III) and CMC, causing film spaces to shrink. Some excessively agglomerated CQDs are too big to fit into the film. Moreover, the complexation between Ce(III) and −NH2 groups on the CQD shell and the carboxyl groups in CMC forms new chemical bonds, achieving crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) [4]. This bonding fixation surpasses intermolecular forces, securely anchoring the CQD within film intermolecular gaps, effectively avoiding CQD direct collisions and achieving solid-state fluorescence (SSF) [51], as shown in Fig. 1(e), depicting TEM image of CQD at high Ce(III) concentration. Interestingly, with a further increase in Ce@CQD concentration, the emission peak of Ce@CQD/CMC maintains, and the FWHM further decreases, with the absorption peak at 416 nm becoming sharper.

We speculate that this is because the unit size of CQD in the film reaches its maximum and does not aggregate. Moreover, due to limited spatial sites, small free fluorescent groups are partially expelled from the site gaps, while some are attached to the surface of CQD [52], making the size of CQD in the film more uniform. The changes in the absorption peak of CQD aq. are described above. Fig. 4(d) shows the fluorescence lifetime curves of the three samples at high concentrations, with CQD aq. having the longest average fluorescence lifetime of 9.35 ns and a PLQY of 15.2 %. In contrast, CQD/CMC has an average fluorescence lifetime of only 4.32 ns and a PLQY of 3.5 %, confirming the occurrence of FRET [51]. However, Ce@CQD/CMC effectively inhibits energy transfer, with an average fluorescence lifetime of 8.01 ns and a PLQY of 14.6 %.

Furthermore, Ce@CQD/CMC also exhibits a decreased Stokes shift with increasing concentration. According to the trap state density theory, this may be due to Ce(III) occupying defective sites on the surface of CQDs, diminishing traps and improving the carrier mobility, which is consistent with the results of increased fluorescence lifetime [53]. However, at the same optical density, CQD/CMC undergoes a severe PL redshift along with an increasing Stokes shift, due to the higher trap density or deeper traps produced by aggregated CQDs [54]. Moreover, as the CQD surface passivation increases, Ce@CQD/CMC displays a narrower bandgap [55], as shown in Fig. 4(c). For photoluminescence, short Stokes shifts can easily lead to reabsorption or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) causing fluorescence quenching [56]. As seen in Fig. 3(g and h), CQD/CMC has absorption and emission peaks with broader FWHM, increasing their overlap area. In contrast, Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits narrower absorption peaks, resulting in a reduced overlap area between the absorption and emission peaks, overcoming the decrease in fluorescence intensity caused by aggregation.

Fig. 3(b) reveals photos of the two films at T550 = 65 % in visible light, where Ce@CQD/CMC has L* = 75.84, a* = 5.26, b* = 53.34, and CQD/CMC has L* = 75.40, a* = 4.56, b* = 48.08, showing both films having comparable brightness and yellowness. Due to the excessive aggregation of CQDs, the numbers observed through the CQD/CMC film appear significantly blurred. In contrast, Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits exceptionally low haze, with clear visibility of the Arabic numbers through the film, indicating good dispersion [23].

Based on this, we maintain a constant CQD concentration, while increasing the Ce(III) concentration. During such manipulation, the CQD concentration decreases and the emission/absorption peaks blue shift, as illustrated in Fig. 4(e), where the absorption peak blue shifts from 471 nm to 448 nm. Two hypotheses for this occur: the quantum confinement effect (QCE) and decreased concentration causing emission to shift to blue [57].

To achieve this, PL emission spectra were obtained at varying excitations for CQD aq., CQD/CMC, and Ce@CQD/CMC at both high and low concentrations. Fig. S4(a and b) detail the PL emission spectra of CQD aq. at high and low concentrations, respectively. At low concentration, the emission of CQD aq. shows no dependence on excitation wavelength, indicating that the luminescence composition at this time is mainly free fluorescent group molecules rather than from the carbon core [52]. Fig. S4(c) depicts the PL emission spectrum of CQD/CMC at low concentration, with the optimal emission wavelength is 451 nm (460 nm in Fig. 3(h)). However, its emission wavelength changes with the excitation wavelength in the same way as shown in Fig. 3(h), commencing at Ex = 390, a red shift in PL emission occurs compared to the initial Ex = 320. This indicates that the luminescent composition of CQD/CMC does not change with the decrease in CQD concentration. Fig. 4(f and g) show the PL emission spectra of Ce@CQD/CMC at high and low CQD concentrations. Interestingly, upon both reaching their maximum excitation wavelengths, the emission peaks exhibit noticeable red shifts, from 462 nm to 472 nm and from 450 nm to 455 nm, espectively. These shifts follow the red shift of the excitation wavelength, showing the emission characteristics of the carbon core. This suggests that the sizes of the luminescent carbon cores are different. Fig. 3(i) speculates on the structural changes of the film following further increased Ce(III) concentration in Ce@CQD/CMC.

Further, modulating of the concentration of Ce(III) is used to regulate the size of CQDs in the film. Fig. 4(h) shows the optimal excitation and emission peaks at different Ce(III) concentrations, with linear fitting applied, i.e. y(Em) = −9.073x + 476.094, R2 = 0.9695, y(Ex) = -21.595x + 430.534, R2 = 0.9602. This indicates that the size of CQDs has a high linear correlation with Ce(III). Fig. 4(i) shows the corresponding CIE coordinate diagram, where the chromaticity of the film changes between cyan and blue depending on its size.

Based on the above analysis, this method regulates the aggregation of CQDs in the Ce@CQD/CMC film by adjusting the concentration of Ce(III). This process can be divided into two stages: When the cross-linking sites in CMC are not fully occupied, CQDs are effectively immobilized in the film through hydrogen bonding with CMC and complexation with Ce(III). As the concentration of Ce@CQDs further increases, there is no excessive aggregation of CQDs. This is because the growth space for CQDs has already been occupied by Ce(III), forming CMC−Ce(III) complex. The amino-shell of CQDs concurrently complexed with Ce(III), preventing direct contact between CQDs, thereby effectively dispersing them in the film through the influence of Ce(III). In instances when the sites in CMC become saturated, there is essentially no further increase in CQDs in the film. At this point, increasing the concentration ratio of Ce(III) compresses the growth space for CQDs, reducing their quantity. Concurrently, larger CQDs are expelled, lowering the average size of CQDs, thus controlling their growth and aggregation through the influence of Ce(III).

Photobleaching resistance governs the lifespan of fluorescent materials. CQD, CQD/CMC, and Ce@CQD/CMC were subjected to UV light (UV-A, 8 W × 2) for 80 h to measure their photostability. Firstly, Fig. 5 (a) demonstrates changes in the fluorescence intensity of CQD aq. Notably, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 34.3 % after 6 h of UV treatment. After around 40 h, it decreased to 8.27 % of the original and plateaued. The photobleaching resistance of CQD is also affected by its concentration too, as shown in Fig. S5(a) for high-concentration CQD aq., which indicates an increase in resistance and a decline in fluorescence intensity to 10.6 % of the original after 80 h, potentially due to the inability of excitation light to reach the interior of the solution [58]. In Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity drop of CQD/CMC is mitigated, holding at 16.5 % of the original post 80 h of UV exposure. However, during this process, the emission peak redshifts and the FWHM increases, generating a new emission at 523 nm. Fluorescence lifetime tests before and after exposure, as shown in Fig. S 5(b), reveal a decrease in CQD/CMC's fluorescence lifetime due to aggregation of CQDs under excitation light. In Fig. 5(c), Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits excellent resistance to photobleaching, maintaining 67.1 % of its original fluorescence intensity after 80 h of exposure, with only about one-third decrease. The fixation of chemical bond effectively suppresses the movement of CQDs caused by the non-radiative recombination [59], without any redshift in the emission peak. Raman and FTIR spectra analysis of CQD/CMC films before and after UV exposure are shown in Fig. 5(e and f). The Raman spectrum shows two bands at 1590 cm−1 and 1335 cm−1, corresponding to sp2-hybridized carbon (G band) and sp3 defects (D band) in CQDs [60], with a decrease in G peak and an increase in D peak after UV irradiation. In the FTIR spectrum, only one peak (3325 cm−1) is shown in the O−H, N−H vibration region after UV irradiation of CQD. The peak at 1023 cm−1 notably rises, indicating an increase in the disordered structure of the film [61]. We speculate that this is due to the partial re-decomposition of the carbon core into N-containing molecular groups, an increase in sp3 defects with the formation of new non-luminescent moieties by N-based molecules under photocatalysis, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Fig. 5(d) shows the UV transmittance curves of the samples, with a decrease in transmittance in the amine absorption segment and an increase in transmittance in the carbon core segment for CQD/CMC. Ce@CQD/CMC, due to more sufficient surface passivation of CQDs, can effectively inhibit the decomposition of the carbon core, thus maintaining higher fluorescence intensity in the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of mechanical properties, thermal stability, and water stability of Ce@CQD/CMC film
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanical properties directly affect the application of the film and excellent mechanical performance is essential. Fig. 6 (a) graphically illustrates the stress–strain relationship of the films, while Fig. 6(b) shows the maximum tensile strength and elongation of the films. Pure CMC forms weak films via hydrogen bonding within its chains and displays poor tensile strength of 27.76 MPa [62]. Upon effective dispersion of CQDs into the CMC film, their hydrophilic functional groups (such as −OH, −COOH, −NH2, etc.) interact with the CMC chains, further increasing the crosslinking density of hydrogen bond within the film [30], significantly enhancing its tensile strength. Moreover, due to the increased hydrogen bond network, the film absorbs more energy under external force [63], enhancing the toughness of CQD/CMC to 7.4 MJ/m3 as shown in Fig. 6(c). Incorporating Ce(III) ions into the film enhances the tensile strength of 0.4Ce@CQD/CMC to 81.68 MPa, exceeding the 54.77 MPa of CQD/CMC significantly due to metal coordination between CMC and Ce(III) [64], which is stronger than hydrogen bonds. Nevertheless, toughness significantly decreases, indicating a shift towards rigidity. In addition, this abrupt drop in strain may be due to the uneven distribution of forces within the film. As increasing Ce(III) concentration, toughness gradually increases again. When the concentration reaches a certain level, due to the limited number of sites, crosslinking gradually saturates, and the tensile strength of film reaches its maximum. Notably, 0.8Ce@CQD/CMC has a higher elastic modulus than 1.2Ce@CQD/CMC (Fig. 6(c)). This may be due to excessive Ce(III) content reducing CQD content and thus affecting film toughness against deformations. Fig. 6(h) shows an image of the dry Ce@CQD/CMC film effortlessly bearing a load of 1 kg (0.5 kg × 2) weight.

The thermal stability of the films was analyzed. Fig. 6(d and e) present TG/DTG curves for the films under a nitrogen atmosphere from 30 °C to 600 °C. Based on the DTG curve, the weight loss of the films mainly occurs in three stages. The first stage, from 110 °C to 196 °C, loss of crystalline water and absorbed water occurs [65]. At this stage, given the significant hydrophilicity of CQD, CQD/CMC films face significant water loss. In addition, the metal−CMC interaction improves the hydrophobicity of Ce@CQD/CMC films, reducing water loss. The second stage occurs between 258 °C and 310 °C, mainly involving the breaking of CMC molecular chains and the decomposition of functional groups [66]. At this stage, due to the Na(I) on the carboxylic functional groups of CMC is replaced by Ce(III) and form complex with CQD, the weight loss of the film occurs earlier (258–274 °C) with the increasing of Ce@CQD content. The third stage involves further decomposition of carbonaceous products into gaseous carbon–oxygen products, occurring after 310 °C [67]. At this stage, the thermal decomposition products further transform into the corresponding metal oxides [68]. Due to the stability of the products, the DTG is relatively low. Notably, the final residual amount of CMC is higher than that of CQD/CMC, 0.4Ce@CQD/CMC, and 0.8Ce@CQD/CMC. This is attributed to the high content of CQDs in the modified film, which further decomposes into gaseous products such as CO2 at high temperatures, thus increasing the rate of weight loss. As the Ce(III) content increases and the CQDs content decreased, the residual amount of CMC for 1.2Ce@CQD/CMC improved. Fig. S6(a and b) illustrate the DSC curves of film samples from 30 to 400 °C, with minor changes in Tg and a higher Tm of CQD/CMC due to CQD addition [69]. UV transparency remained unaffected in both films (Fig. 6(f)) with a reduction at near 300 nm, hypothesized to result from carbon core–shell decomposition generating free N-based functional groups. Fig. 6(g) shows the PL changes in the two films, with no significant change in the emission peak and fluorescence intensity of Ce@CQD/CMC before and after heating, while CQD/CMC experienced an increase in emission peak at wavelengths above 550 nm. After dehydration at 150 °C, CQD/CMC films might have intense CQD aggregation due to partially solidified CQDs trapping some water, causing a significant redshift of the emission peak. Briefly, Ce@CQD/CMC exhibits better heat resistance up to 150 °C over CQD/CMC. Fig. 6(i) shows the load-bearing capacity of the Ce@CQD/CMC film after wetting. The film can still withstand 1 kg of weight under the surrounding water molecules, indicating good water stability. Fig. 6(j) shows the water processing ability of the Ce@CQD/CMC film. Under moist conditions, the two ends of the elongated film are fused and form a closed circular ring within minutes. More importantly, the resulting thin film that formed a circular ring can still easily withstand the weight of 1 kg of weight. The connection has not been torn open again, indicating that the interface is in a well-sealed state, as shown in Fig. 6(k).

The water resistance of film materials can limit their application in various environments. Moreover, among various solvents, water often has high polarity, so having good water resistance is crucial. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the contact angle of CQD/CMC is lower than that of CMC, because the surface of CQD has a large number of hydrophilic functional groups [70], avoiding reducing the hydrophobicity of the film formed by general doping methods.

Fig. 7(b) shows images of CMC, CQD/CMC, and Ce@CQD/CMC (from top to bottom) after immersion in water for 3 h. Even pure CMC remains residual after soaking, while CQD/CMC film dissolves completely in water. Due to the metal coordination bond of a Ce (III) with CMC on the surface of Ce@CQD/CMC, a non-polar region was formed and water was more efficiently repelled than CQD/CMC [71]. Hydrated layers were formed via hydrogen bonds, reducing the interactions of nonpolar molecule, thus increasing the water contact angle at Ce@CQD/CMC. In addition, as the concentration of Ce(III) increases (from 0 to 1.2), the non-polar regions on the film surface become more densely packed, resulting in an increase in the water contact angle of the film. Fig. 7(c) displays the swelling rate of the film samples, which parallels the outcomes of the water contact angle. Owing to the hydrophilic nature of CQDs, the swelling rate of the CQD/CMC film is further elevated. In contrast, with the incorporation of Ce(III), the swelling rate experienced a significant reduction, particularly in the cases of 0.8Ce@CQD/CMC and 1.2Ce@CQD/CMC, which exhibit swelling rates of only about 583 % and 442 %, respectively, and show a trend towards stabilization. Interestingly, the water vapor permeability (WVP) of CQD/CMC is still lower than that of CMC, as shown in Fig. 7(d). This may be due to the intricate and complicated cross-linked network effectively delaying the diffusion of water vapor [72]. The film samples were tested in a constant temperature and humidity chamber at RH = 90 %, 10 °C. After 24 h, the CQD/CMC film became damp, and the film collapsed severely due to the influence of water molecules (Fig. 7(i)), exhibiting a high elongation (about 65.4 %) and drastic decline in tensile strength, merely 5.2 MPa. Notably, under high humidity, CQD/CMC increases its fluorescence intensity due to an increased polarity within the film, enhancing dispersion of CQDs and reducing aggregate-induced quenching (ACQ). However, the decrease in the refractive index also results in a loss of UV blocking efficiency, as shown in Fig. 7(e and g). In Ce@CQD/CMC, the CQDs are completely encapsulated by Ce(III), so their performance does not change significantly.

Firstly, based on the anchoring of CQDs by Ce(III) and CMC, we designed a copper ion film probe. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), solutions of various metal ions (10 μM) were selected to evaluate the selectivity of the film. The results revealed that the fluorescence intensity was highest adding Cu2+ ions, exceeding 2.1, demonstrating the excellent anti-interference ability of the film. Remarkably, unlike most Cu2+ fluorescence probes [73], this film functions as an “ON” type probe. Fig. 8(b) shows the PL emission spectra at different Cu2+ concentrations, where the fluorescence intensity of the film is enhanced in the range of 0−10 μM. Fig. S7(a) shows the fluorescence lifetime and FTIR of the film before and after adding Cu2+ ions. After adding Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence lifetime of the film extends from 8.01 ns to 8.76 ns. In the FTIR, the peaks at 1423 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric peaks of COO− [74], both shifting after adding Cu2+ ions.

Based on the above analysis, we speculate that the working mechanism of the probe is similar to that of Ce(III). The atomic radius of Cu(II) is small, but there are still some vacant cross-linking sites in CMC and CQDs, and Cu2+ ions has stronger adsorption effect, so Cu2+ can be adsorbed by CMC, and further separate CQDs to fill their surface defects [75]. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of the probe increases. Further linearity between Cu2+ ions and fluorescence intensity is displayed in Fig. 8(c and d). These exhibit high linearity across the ranges 0–0.8 μM and 1–10 μM, with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.16 μM and 0.37 μM, respectively. Thus, the working principle of the probe may be that the atomic radius of Cu is smaller than that of Ce3+, and the carboxylic groups in CMC have a stronger coordination effect with Cu2+ than Ce3+, ensuring that Cu2+ coordinates with carboxylic groups in CMC and forms new crosslinking bonds to separate some CQDs and further fill their surface defects [75], thereby increasing the fluorescence intensity of the film.

Interestingly, upon reaching 0.1 M Cu2+, the film's fluorescence declines, as seen in Fig. S7(c), potentially due to high competition between Cu2+ and Ce3+ ions for amine sites, leading to Cu2+ ions displacement and CQD surface defects, triggering electron extraction and quenching. Further linear fitting between the Cu2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity is performed, as shown in Fig. 8(c and d), exhibiting a high linearity in the ranges of 0–0.8 μM and 1–10 μM, with their detection limits (LOD) being 0.16 μM and 0.37 μM, respectively. Notably, when the concentration of Cu2+ reaches above 0.1 M, the fluorescence intensity of the film declines, as shown in Fig. S7(c), potentially due to high coordination competition between Cu2+ and Ce3+ ions for amine sites, leading to the large-scale substitution of Ce3+ ions with Cu2+ and thus increasing surface defects of CQDs, causing electrons to be taken away by a large number of copper ions, and triggering a quenching phenomenon [76]. Fig. 8(e) shows the photos of Ce@CQD/CMC as a ion probe film under ultraviolet light before and after the addition of Cu2+ solution. Secondly, the CMC used as the film substrate is a cellulose derivative with abundant hydroxyl and carboxylic groups that not only form a hydrogen bond crosslinking network, but also undergo further bonding and functionalization through external interactions [62]. In this work, CMC reacted with rare earth metals to form a modified film that preserved the CMC−Ce backbone in the surrounding water molecules and maintained the water self-healing properties of cellulose [77]. Moreover, by using the water-adhesion of the film, large pieces of Ce@CQD/CMC can be adhered to glass windows (Fig. 8(g)) without additional glue, achieving near-invisible high transparency. A three-dimensional comparison was made with the UV shielding film reported in recent literature, as shown in Fig. 8(h), where the x-axis represents the UV blocking efficiency at 400 nm (E400), the y-axis represents the total UV blocking efficiency in the UVA-C range (EUVA-C), and the z-axis represents T550. In this work, the film as a novel UV-shielding film achieves complete UV shielding (E400 = 98.16 %, EUVA-C=99.77 %) while still exhibiting ultra-high visible light transmittance (T550 = 88.21 %), which is not usually found in traditional UV-shielding films [15].

Furthermore, the film can also act as a “fluorescent water sticker”, adhering to objects with water as an adhesive. Under visible light, the sticker is nearly undetectable by the naked eyes, but under ultraviolet light, a distinct pattern emerges on the glass (Fig. 8(i)). The film also has good luminescent stability, as shown in Fig. 8(j), emitting bright fluorescence in polar water molecules even after a week of UV exposure, showing its potential for anti-counterfeit applications.

In addition, the film can store encrypted information. As shown in Fig. 8(k), a high concentration cerium ion solution is used as the “invisible ink,’ and the letters of “LT” are written on the surface of the film. After the ink dries, the film remains colorless and transparent. At this point, the handwriting is exposed under UV light. The addition of cerium ions greatly compresses the space of CQDs, and the large number of cerium ions covering the surface of the film surround and compete with CQDs for electrons, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity.

Based on the control of CQD size by the film, we further developed a dual-encrypted “bracelet,” as shown in Fig. 8(l). This bracelet assembled from the self-healing property of the Ce@CQD/CMC film is completely transparent under white light illumination. Under 395 nm UV light irradiation, the “bracelet” emits bright fluorescence but does not display any information. When the UV wavelength is switched to 380 nm, the butterfly pattern on the “bracelet” appears. The butterfly pattern is printed with a high Ce(III) concentration Ce@CQD solution. The CQDs emitting light at Ex = 395 nm are larger in size, with their chromaticity CIE coordinates shown in Fig. 4(i). When the excitation wavelength changes to 380 nm, the smaller CQDs in the butterfly pattern reach their optimal excitation wavelength, emitting deep blue fluorescence. At the same time, the relative fluorescence intensity of the larger CQDs decreases, and the butterfly pattern can be displayed.

Based on the unique properties of carboxymethylcellulose, we have successfully fabricated Ce@CQD/CMC films using a simple, green and sustainable self-assembly method for encapsulation of carbon dots, which is in line with the viewpoint of social sustainable development. Because CQDs are fixed in the film by chemical bonds, so compared to general solid-state carbon dot doping, this film not only effectively prevents the quenching caused by high-concentration aggregation of CQDs, but also ensures low haze, making it an effective transparent luminescent material. With the spatial restriction of metal ions on CQDs, the film can serve as a Cu2+ indicator (0–10 μM) and for encrypted data storage. Moreover, varying the concentration of Ce(III) in Ce@CQD/CMC allows the size of CQDs to be controlled by the film, alleviating the problem of uneven size during synthesis. Based on the different excitation wavelengths due to different sizes of CQDs, a film with dual information encryption was developed. In addition, the Ce@CQD/CMC film in this work also has good acid-base stability (pH=2–12), strong resistance to photobleaching (the fluorescence intensity only drops to 1/3 of the original after 80 h of UV exposure), high mechanical strength (87.38 MPa), good thermal stability, and other advantages. As a UV shielding film, the film not only achieves complete UV shielding (E400 = 98.16 %, EUVA-C = 99.77 %), but also has extremely high visible light transmittance (T550 = 88.21 %), which is significantly superior to other films with similar functions. Finally, using the hydrogen bond self-healing properties of cellulose, the film is assembled and water-adhered for practical applications. In short, we believe that this smart thin film composed of carbon dot and carboxymethylcellulose has great potential in fields such as information security, UV protection, and metal ion detection. These characteristics could extend the horizons of solid-state carbon dots.

Zhihao Shan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiayi Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Yuling Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Yuping Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Yiqun Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
